https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3ijLuqb1: Launching 3D Jumpstart — in-person CAD and 3D printing classes for grades 4–12 in North Suwanee, built on what worked teaching 50+ students at Artios Academies. Students learn Fusion 360, the same professional software engineers use, then run real printers to hold their designs in their hands. Small groups, real tools, no game-playing dressed up as STEM. Promo spot for the launch — enrollment open at 3djumpstart.com.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WySt0ltF: Beaver's Bathroom Blitz is a browser game where you run the cleaning crew at a Buc-ee's-style mega-stop — plunge toilets, scrub stalls, dodge the health inspector, and serve VIP customers across six escalating shifts. I built the full thing: design, code, art direction, and the absurd premise. Survive all six and you walk away with the Golden Plunger. Playable now at beaversbathroomblitz.com.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=A3V_AN36: A walkthrough of SafeTunes, the Apple Music parental-control overlay I built after Apple's own filters kept letting explicit tracks slip past my kids. I demo the full loop — kid requests a song with a six-digit family code, parent reviews lyrics with an AI assist, approves or blocks, then curates a Discover tab so kids can explore artists like Elvis, Forrest Frank, or John Williams without anything getting forced on them. I also show the blocked-search notifications that turn a sketchy search into a parent-kid conversation, plus the fix for Apple's other quiet problem: inappropriate album artwork on otherwise clean records. Whitelist-only music, built for families who want the actual final say.
https://drive.google.com/file/d/1igFp99E: Raised a 12-person hunting cabin on Mike Carney's farm using Amish-style timber framing — no cranes, just eight guys, gin poles, and a lot of weight we underestimated. Pegged the joinery with 150-year-old locust pegs slicked with soap to seat them, an inch on the bottom and an inch and an eighth on top. Caught the whole thing on camera, including the moment the frame nearly got away from us.
https://drive.google.com/file/d/17bf9a3n: Hand-deconstructing a pre-1850s mountain barn to rebuild it as a cabin for the Dirty Dozen — every board pulled gently, every nail backed out by hand because the originals were valuable enough that settlers once burned houses just to recover them. Salvaged a rare livestock windlass on the way out, the kind of pulley rig you almost never see intact. Tight clock, fragile material, no shortcuts. Watch the barn come apart one nail at a time.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cQRgMfAq: Fusion 360 walkthrough I built for my High School Creative Technologies class — sketch a 2.5-inch construction square on the right plane, rough out a profile with a backward lean, a front lip, and a sloped back, then extrude two inches into a printable phone stand. Along the way I cover switching default units to inches, converting sketch lines to construction guides, and using the timeline to jump back and patch an open profile before extruding. Beginner-friendly, no fluff, the kind of homework that actually prints.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3uTHHkXF: A three-film tour through the dawn of cinema — Méliès' A Trip to the Moon (1902), Porter's The Great Train Robbery (1903), and Griffith's Intolerance (1916) — and why each one rewrote the rulebook. Special effects, cross-cutting, on-location shooting, Babylonian sets the size of city blocks: the grammar of every movie you've ever loved started here. All three are free on YouTube, which means there's no excuse not to watch them after this.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AyAAfqjc: Bought a quality tripod at a film school's going-out-of-business sale, but the camera plate was a hair too wide for the dovetail and a replacement ran $125. So I clamped it in the Bridgeport, set up a 30-degree angle block to mirror the 60-degree dovetail, and shaved it down with a half-inch four-flute end mill in light passes. Slid it home, locked it in — perfect fit, $125 saved. This is why I keep a mill in the shop.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fJHMbqUd: Indie psychological thriller where a new pastor and his wife move into a controlling church matriarch's isolated North Georgia home while the parsonage is renovated — and the previous pastor's death starts looking less like a gas leak. Owes more to Psycho and Misery than to anything in the faith-film aisle, with a sheltered adult son, fourteen rattlesnakes, and the only working phone hidden in a chicken house. Tense, dryly funny, and genre-confident — small-town church politics played as a slow-burn stalker movie. Watch how fast the welcome curdles.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OPp40gDl: Short narrative film about a boy convinced a Haunted Mansion ghost followed him home after a day at Disneyland, and the new woman in his dad's life who has to face it with him. Directed follow-up to Missing in the Mansion (2012), starring Jesse Corti — the voice of LeFou in Beauty and the Beast — alongside Zachary Haven, Tara Battani, and Rick Marcus. Practical scares, a fractured family finding its footing, and an original score available at followedfromthemansion.com. Stay for the flashlight.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=N1CslPue: Intro to Creative Technology students designed custom chocolate bar molds from scratch — modeling in Fusion 360, 3D printing the bucks, vacuum forming PETG sheets, then casting the chocolate themselves. End-to-end CAD-to-candy in one project, built to give high schoolers real reps with the tools of a creative career. Watch them eat their homework.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0M-yetCG: A found-footage horror short staged inside Disneyland's Haunted Mansion, where a Club 33 proposal and an Imagineering internship audition unravel into a disappearance caught on a single camera left in Lost and Found. I co-wrote and shot it guerrilla-style on the live attraction, leaning on the ride's own scares and a local legend about a child's ashes spread on the track. Three friends went missing on July 6, 2012 — this is the tape they left behind.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QKcbGbaj: Behind-the-scenes breakdown of how I shot Missing in the Mansion guerrilla-style inside Disneyland — no permits, no cast member help, no park rules broken. Empty ride vehicles came from first-thing-in-the-morning timing across roughly a dozen Haunted Mansion laps over two to three days. The doom buggy pull was faked in-studio with a flower pot, paper plates for speakers, and a lot of rehearsal. The Hatbox Ghost and Joshua were shot on green screen and composited in — including a two-and-a-half-hour makeup chair sit to turn an actor into a 90-year-old Slash.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AkNDTLnJ: Day-one vlog from the parking lot of Oconee Fall Line Technical College — 8 a.m., January 11, three days after quitting a video producer gig at Walt Disney World. I traded Orlando and the commercial work for a small Georgia town and a machining program, betting that a few years of self-taught making was enough signal to go all in. No wife, no kids, age 40, probably the oldest guy in the class. Camera on, nerves up, walking in.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FTHXP9uq: A classroom-style breakdown of how to design custom threads in Fusion 360 for 3D printing — covering the Thread Tool, Offset Tool, and the fit tolerances that decide whether your part actually screws together off the print bed. I open with a quick history and the science of how threads convert rotation to linear motion, then walk through Imperial thread naming, pitch gauges, taps and dies, and why coarse threads hold up better in plastic. Built for my high school Creative Technologies students, but useful for any maker who's tired of printing threads that strip on the first turn. If you've ever guessed at a 3/8-16 callout, this one's for you.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JC4lM6Cj: Custom lightsaber hilt design tutorial in Fusion 360, built for my high school Creative Technologies class. I walk through sketching the 10-inch profile, locking constraints, and using Revolve to spin up the base hilt — then dig into the Pattern and Emboss tools to add ridges, buttons, and emitter details around the body. Sized to print on a standard 10-inch bed, so students can take it from CAD to a real prop. If you're new to Fusion, this is the build that makes the rest of CAD click.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qks9lAGC: Fusion 360 tutorial built for my high school Creative Technologies class — a hands-on walkthrough of the Loft, Sweep, and Revolve tools. I model three projects from scratch in inches: a soda-can mug sized to half a Coke can's diameter with a quarter-inch base, a paperclip, and a vase. Along the way I cover fit-point splines, profile selection, axis snapping, and how to tune curvature so the bottom sits flat. If you've never revolved a profile before, this is the one that finally makes it click.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mQj5A3DI: Fusion 360 walkthrough modeling a part from a blueprint — sketch on the bottom plane, two-point rectangle, dimensioned extrude, then a cut-extrude to carve the stepped profile. Covers fillets on all four corners, the Project tool for pulling reference geometry into a new sketch, and construction-line diagonals to nail dead center for a through-hole. Built for my high school Creative Technologies class as a clean intro to reading prints and translating them into CAD.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TYCITPU8: No-guide caving trip through Pettyjohn's Cave with my brothers and nephews for my birthday — paper map, headlamps, and zero safety net. Twenty minutes in, the sole of my boot tore loose, and I spent the next hour squeezing through icy-slick crawls and hobbit-hole tunnels on a flapping shoe. Salamanders, stalagmites, a Budweiser can older than me, and one wrong turn from being a true crime podcast cold open. Watch us figure out whether we actually find the way back out.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jC7Y8g2P: Built oversized Lumiere candles — head and both hands — for a high school production of Beauty and the Beast. Polycam-scanned the actor's head, sculpted the wax drips in Nomad Sculpt, added handles and button cutouts in Fusion 360, then printed in ABS, primed, and finished in cream satin. Wired each piece to a 9V with an inline switch so the actor could trigger the LED flames as character beats — soldered backstage during tech week.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=G9IbnyaH: I built a set of nesting apple boxes for the film classes I teach — pancake, quarter, half, and full — modeled in Fusion 360 and machined the hand-hold pockets on a Tormach CNC mill. Cut sheet goods with a Makita track saw, dialed in the dado grooves so the smaller boxes slide out and nest inside the full apple, then laser-engraved the school's logo on each one. Lessons learned: spring for five-ply Baltic birch, not the three-ply I cheaped out on.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0G3zJUQ9: I built a Steadicam dolly sled for a Hollywood grip crew, designed in Fusion 360 around a client-supplied cheese plate and fabricated from 5/8-inch steel plate I had craned into the back of a Honda Accord. Every edge got squared on the Bridgeport, 25 wheel-mount holes drilled and countersunk, and the whole frame MIG-welded with a Fireball Mega Square keeping it true. Mid-build the client and I cut weight by re-machining all four wheel assemblies thinner — same process, four more times. It's heading to set on a major studio production, and yes, I built it in my Honda.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XiI7-xkY: Bent an 8-foot diameter conduit ring for a Midsummer Night's Dream set piece — first time using a pipe bender, four 10-foot sticks, four 75.4-inch segments, nine 10-degree bends each, marked every 8 and 3/8 inches per the MakerPipe calculator. Salvaged an over-bent crease, coupled the quarter circles, and closed the ring outside in the cold. Then did what anyone would do with a fresh 8-foot metal hoop — rolled it like a giant hula hoop in the driveway.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=z5cNOhk6: One-day build of a tiny aluminum 3D printer adapter plate, start to finish on a Tormach 1100 MX. Toolpaths in Fusion 360, seven tools staged — face mill, quarter-inch and eighth-inch end mills, a 1/16th for the inner pocket, spot drill, tap drill, tap. Total cut time: six minutes. The smallest part I've ever machined, and the setup takes longer than the chips fly.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3EnLWNNS: Machined replacement parts for an 80-year-old WWII German Mauser bayonet release after a re-enactor friend brought me the broken original. Used both Tormach machines in tandem for the first time — CNC mill for the body off a Fusion 360 toolpath, lathe for the button — running a die for the metric threads since I haven't tackled thread milling yet. The smallest part I've ever cut, and a carbon copy of an 80-year-old piece of history.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=l8OA1bmn: Built a pair of portable skewer holders to end the backyard campfire chaos — no more sticky forks in the dirt or marshmallow rods underfoot. Ground and welded 316 stainless rod into offset hooks on driven stakes, then turned oak handles on the lathe and finished them with stain, urethane, and five-minute epoxy. Simple shop problem, simple shop answer — grinder, bender, bandsaw, MIG, lathe. Stick two in the ground, stack your skewers, eat the s'more.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qAV7_GMj: Designed and fabricated a winch-driven hinge rig in Fusion 360 for a local church's Easter production — a wheeled cage that captures the base of the cross, pivots at the cart's end, and safely raises and lowers an actor playing Jesus. Custom problem-solving from sketch to weld, built to perform live in front of a congregation. A tangible answer to the question of whether the career change was going to work.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9xzzEIro: Machined 15 archery tag arrows for my nephews using a Tormach CNC lathe, a Bridgeport mill, and a CO2 laser cutter. Designed the Delrin arrowheads in Fusion 360, ran two ops on the lathe, turned aluminum nocks, then notched them on the Bridgeport with a 5C collet block. Laser-cut foam rings for the padding, JB Welded everything to the shafts, and finished with hockey tape and a Flex Seal coat for the ones that needed to survive a backyard war. First prototype hit me in the chest — didn't hurt, kid grinned, project greenlit.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0s1u5tY5: Time-lapse of an entire machine shop lathe bay getting torn down and rebuilt to make room for three new LeBlond lathes at Lanier Technical College, where I spent a year studying machining. Most of the heavy moves are my instructor Tommy Morris on the forklift — hours of rigging compressed into a few minutes. Shot multi-cam on iPhone, GoPro 360 Max, Hero 9, and the original GoPro White.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Clu_oJa6: CNC-machined aluminum labels for the material rack at Lanier Technical College's machine shop, solving the thin-stock workholding problem with the superglue-and-painters-tape trick from John Saunders at NYC CNC. Spot-drilled the holes, engraved the text seven thousandths deep, profiled the perimeter, and chamfered the edges — all without a vise. Finished by flooding the surface with Dykem, sanding it back so the red dye only sits in the engraved letters, then riveting the labels to the rack. A small shop-floor upgrade that doubled as a workholding masterclass.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2U0sE2Je: Built a wall-mounted skateboard rack in under an hour to stop the boards from rolling under cars in the garage — one had already been run over. Laid out the spacing against the smallest deck, marked 45-degree notches, then roughed them on the band saw, finished the curves with a Forstner bit on the drill press, and cleaned up on a spindle sander. Glue, screws, three cross pieces, studs found, level held by my nephew Henry. Decks off the floor, artwork on display, problem solved.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J4g10RYo: Full teardown and rebuild of a 1970s Bridgeport milling machine, repainted in a custom Iron Man/Stark Industries scheme. Stripped to bare castings with a grinder, flap disc, and wire wheel inside a homemade dust booth, then bagged, cleaned, and oiled every part for reassembly. One year into machining and now running a refurbished Bridgeport that works on every axis. Closer to a love letter to the machine's history than a how-to.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7FmV7olF: Added a bar puller to my Tormach 15L Slant Pro and turned a manual feed job into lights-out production. The job: 100 spacers for a stock race car, sitting between the fiberglass shell and the steel frame. Loaded three feet of bar stock, cut a custom bushing on the manual lathe to damp the spin, hit go, and walked away while the collet pulled, locked, and cut 36 parts in a run. First real taste of automation in the shop — terrifying for about ten seconds, then addictive.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5afOA2XU: Designed and fabricated a custom adjustable steel mount for a friend's center channel speaker — no off-the-shelf bracket existed for hanging it above a projector screen. Mocked the design in Shapr3D, cut stock on the bandsaw, drilled pivot points, and MIG welded the frame, then heat-and-hammered a warped corner back into spec. Final build has hand-tightened pivots, neoprene-lined contact points, an adjustable rear angle bracket, and zip-tie earthquake bracing for the client's California install. Watch a paper pattern turn into a painted steel mount that fits the speaker like it was poured around it.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8mNohMez: Designed and welded the steel base for an extendable farmhouse table built to seat all 16 members of my extended family. Modeled the frame in Fusion 360, planned cuts with OptiCutter, ran an Evolution 14" metal chop saw for clean 45s, and turned custom bench dogs on a Tormach Slant Pro 15L CNC lathe to square work on the welding table. First MIG project in the home shop with a Lincoln 210 on 75/25 — built a matching bench first to dial the process, then scaled up to legs, cross members, and pull-out wing extensions that actually slide. Family build with my dad, brother, and sister-in-law on the woodwork; metal base was mine.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r-I8oaUL: Machined a replica of the Inception spinning top totem on a Tormach Slant Pro 15L CNC lathe — traced the prop from a film still in Fusion 360, spun the profile around an axis, and ran the CAM file in stainless steel. Part of a trade-school pivot from a producer gig at Disney to learning lathes and mills, with a few epic-fail chips along the way. Stainless because aluminum would've dinged the first time it hit the floor. Stick around for the wake-or-dream test at the end.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=prJ3259d: Heat treated and surface ground a pair of 1-2-3 blocks down to one inch by two inch by three inch — accurate to the ten thousandths — for my final project at Lanier Technical College. Engraved and numbered the blanks on a CNC mill, sealed them in stainless foil for the heat treat oven, then worked them across three different grinders: an NC grinder, a manual, and a semi-auto. Verified with gauge blocks, a dial indicator, and a CMM for parallelism and perpendicularity. Eight months out of Disney and into trade school — turns out I like surface grinding more than I expected.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_vs9JLMv: Unboxed, hauled, and assembled a Tormach 15L Slant Pro CNC lathe in my home shop — three pallets, an 18-wheeler that couldn't make the driveway, a borrowed box truck, an engine hoist, and a lot of adult Legos. Walked through the upgrades I added: air-powered 5C collet closer, touchscreen monitor, and the automatic tool changer turret. Then powered her up, jogged the X and Z, and turned my first computer-controlled part out of aluminum. First step into CNC machining — and the start of where this shop is headed.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g0r61RY2: Built out a new Metro Atlanta workshop from scratch — three eight-foot anchored workbenches modeled in Shaper3D, ten 5,000-lumen LEDs swapped in for the old fluorescents, and a full top-to-bottom reorg with a retractable extension cord and shop TV. Designed the benches with a leg overhang to fix the comfort problem from my last shop, used $4 whiteboard panels for the shelving, and soldered the braided-to-solid light wiring myself. Three days of build, one mid-project swim break when both drill batteries died, and a chaos-to-order time lapse to prove it. A place for everything — and finally room to make.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=huDBDSMO: First stick welding (SMAW) assignment in Intro to Welding — laying beads into an X-shaped coupon, aka "the Xbox." I run through 6010 with the whip-and-pause method, then 7018 for smoother filler passes, plus a first run on a metal break that stamps quarter-inch steel like paper. Honest ranking so far: flux core on top, stick second, oxy fuel last — MIG and TIG still to come. Trade school, real sparks, no master-welder pretense.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Tg6Nna--: Trade school update from Lanier Tech in metro Atlanta — second semester running heat treatment, surface grinding, intro welding, mechanical physics, and small business management. Quenched parts at 1500 degrees in oil that occasionally lit up, sandblasted the gunk off, then ground to a tolerance of plus or minus three ten-thousandths and landed one ten-thousandth under. Also got a first crack at oxyfuel welding with acetylene — tough, but a blast. Shot and cut on iPad Pro in LumaFusion while building out a new workshop from scratch.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=C47pf1lI: Built a five-axis work stop from stainless and aluminum as my final project after one semester of trade school — lathe work, fly-cutting, rotary-table curves, slot cutter, boring head, the whole manual workflow. The instructor watched me grind on the curved aluminum pieces for days, then mentioned a CNC could spit the same part out in five minutes. That's the moment I decided where I'm headed next. Honest take on starting over at 40, ruined parts and all.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ThRcnSOk: Two trade school machining projects: a Morse taper center drill holder for my home lathe, and a boring/press-fit exercise sized a thousandth oversize for an arbor press. The taper job meant recutting the print from a Morse 3 down to a Morse 2 to fit my setup. The press fit got humbling fast — the arbor press wasn't bolted down, so I bailed to a hydraulic press in the diesel shop to finish the job.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ypmAXIZg: Two classes deep into machinist school — Lathe 1 and Mill 1 — and the projects pile up fast: a 1-2-3 block, an octagonal cup holder cut on a rotary table, a steel strap clamp, and a thread-cutting bar with matching left- and right-hand nuts. Then the real win: my home lathe tailstock snapped mid-project, and instead of buying a replacement, I remade the broken part from scratch — turning, threading, and cutting a keyway on the mill to lock the handle to the drive bar. First time machining solved a problem instead of creating one. It dropped right back in and works good as new.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oit9VQPI: I built four rolling work tables sized to drop over Uline basket trucks for Jambos, a nonprofit that gives brand-new pajamas to kids entering foster care. Family shop project — measured 26x36, milled the tops, and rolled them straight into volunteer Saturday where we sorted a literal mountain of pajamas off hangers. Shot on GoPro 9, cut on iPad Pro in LumaFusion. Stick around for the niece cameo and the moment one of the carts gets put to work.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=81GvsCm0: First project out of MILL I at trade school — a 1-2-3 block machined from raw stock after leaving a Disney producer gig to learn the craft hands-on. Face cutting, end milling, drilling, reaming, tapping — all new muscle memory, all on camera. Cut on iPad Pro in LumaFusion. Stick around for the moment it comes off the mill clean.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IAQ5vq0F: Built out my Georgia workshop wall by wall — insulating, paneling, and framing a 36-inch workbench sized to the window line so the room reads seamless. Modeled the bench in Shapr3d on iPad Pro before cutting, rigged Loc-line camera arms off the rafters (Adam Savage tip), and sized the under-shelf to fit Walmart storage bins exactly. First real surface in the shop — no more building on my knees in the driveway.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LSzTUvN3: I built a custom doggie door for my parents' three beagles — frame out of PVC for weatherproofing, magnet-and-paint-pen alignment for a clean close, marine-grade vinyl flaps after a floor-mat first pass. Got the outside stairs and a landing in too, since the dogs had to clear a windowsill before the drop. The build dragged — wrong glue, family complaints about the noise, a full v2 with corrugated sign-board frames and rotating flaps — but it actually works. Watch a beagle named Ruthie shove her way through and decide if it was worth the months.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rXRRglYT: Cut my first proper threads on a lathe in machinist school — internal and external, nuts and bolts to spec. Once you find the rhythm it's weirdly meditative: nudge in, pass, back out, repeat. Now a Home Depot run hits different — I can read a quarter-inch 20 UNC and go make the mate from scratch. Threads are just power transfer in disguise, and that idea is wildly cool.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=M2e7WjiL: Packed up my first personal workshop after three and a half years — paint station, electronics bench, CNC, 3D printer, foam cutter, welding corner, and a table saw built into its own outfeed. Florida townhome sold, so the shop got broken down, the scrap pile got handed to the junkers, and the truck pointed north to Georgia for trade school. A farewell tour of the old shop, a family packing montage, and one very enthusiastic cameo from my brother plugging his channel mid-load.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZzpkO-80: Wired the first breaker and outlet in my new Georgia workshop — drilling through studs in a brand-new building, running 12/2 to a 15-amp lighting circuit with 20-amp branches planned for the power tools. Not an electrician, but the lights came on and nobody got electrocuted. Mini lathe is in, outlet boxes are roughed around the walls, and the Florida shop move is next.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m_Iq18xY: Brought in a licensed electrician to run a 90-amp service to the new workshop — enough headroom for 220V, a welder, and the heavier machinery I've been waiting to plug in. Wiring the outlets myself, which I've never done before, then insulation goes in so it stops feeling like a walk-in freezer. Old shop's still half-packed and the new one's a mess, but power first, everything else follows.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2xvhf6a: Custom-built workshop delivered to my new place in Georgia — a portable shop I can take with me wherever I land next. Nephew Hudson rolled GoPro on the drop while I scoped out the bones: solid storage up top, pre-built shelving, room to grow. First moves are insulating against the Georgia cold and framing in workbenches. Watch the build start from an empty box.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DG43Eo4F: I built a Ninja Turtles sewer diorama for my bookshelf using XPS insulation foam, a Proxxon hot-wire cutter, and a Shiftinglands circle cutter to carve brick walls, miter-cut tunnels, and straw pipework. The finish is layered — Mod Podge and black paint base, gray gesso pushed into the grout lines, syringe-applied washes for grime, airbrushed graffiti, Sculptamold for corner buildup, and silver Rub 'n Buff on the pipes. Three LEDs wired in series sell the moonlight-through-the-grate moment. Donatello, Raph, Leo, Mikey, and Casey Jones move in at the end.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8EHbTShv: First successful thread-cutting pass on a metal lathe — single-point threading a brass rod after months of binge-watching Blondihacks, Click Spring, and Adam Savage. Setup, infeed, and that satisfying moment the nut spins on clean. New shop skill unlocked, and I'm already eyeing the next project.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-FROujJI: I built JPL and NASA's open-source face-touch warning pendant — resin-printed the casing, soldered up the IR sensor, transistor, buzzer, switch, and battery, then crammed it all into the housing. First unit buzzed on cue once I dialed in the sensitivity screw; second one came together in a time-lapse. Not glamorous footage — just a lot of soldering and a working build at the end.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BEVPKR5D: Built a Montessori floor bed from scratch for a coworker's daughter after Googling what one even was. SketchUp for the cut list, Forstner bit and drill press for the dowel holes, a cross-slide vise for clean repeatable positioning, and lag screws at the corners so it breaks down to fit in my car. Roughly 60 hours, $119 in materials, plus a scroll saw and cross-slide vise added to the shop along the way. Includes the failed jig, the racked panel I had to remake, and the moment the drill press fixed everything I'd been doing the hard way.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Gz4p3gsm: First attempt at milling — adapted a drill press with a cross-slide vise and end mills to machine a custom lock-down part in high-density plastic, learning from YouTube and figuring it out on the fly. Shot and cut on an iPhone 11 Pro Max with FILMIC Pro and LumaFusion. The part works. The learning curve is just getting started.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9Ip6WS7c: Built two custom-sized picture frame shelves in a day with a miter saw, Forstner bit, glue, and a nail gun — sized to fit a wall where store-bought options came up short. Learned the hard way that skipping the clamps on a cupped board means the shelf won't sit right, then flipped the assembly order on the second one and nailed it. Pre-stained to dodge blotchiness, finished them up, and handed them off to friends who hung and styled them better than I could have. Shot on an iPhone 11 Pro Max, cut on iPad Pro in LumaFusion, no music — just the tools.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oy27ugri: Welded a found-object robot for my nephew after he spotted one at a Georgia flea market and commissioned me on the spot — with the condition that I make a YouTube video about it. Built from nuts, bolts, spacers, cable ties, and electrical connectors, tacked together with a flux core welder, then weathered with brown and black washes for that been-in-battle look. First attempt at found object art — landed somewhere between IG-88 and the Rocketeer. Shot on iPhone 11 Pro Max, cut in LumaFusion on iPad Pro.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GlviIu9h: Built a wall-mounted garden hose hanger from reclaimed pine 2x4s pulled out of a dumpster. Five minutes, zero hardware-store runs, fully functional. Shot on iPhone 11 Pro Max, cut in LumaFusion on iPad Pro. Quick shop update — scrap in, useful object out.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PTrUFPgc: Built a mitered frame for a 12x24 poster out of 1x4s — ripped to 2 inches on the table saw, notched the back for poster and plexiglass, then watched the glue-up dry out of square. Recut the corners on the bandsaw, cleaned them up on the miter saw, and clamped the second glue-up flat to the table so it actually stayed square. Shot on iPhone 11 Pro Max, edited in LumaFusion on iPad Pro. A short, honest look at fixing a mistake instead of starting over.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lehhURck: Built a rideable backyard train from scratch for my nieces and nephews — welded frame, sprocket-and-chain drive, 48V battery pack, 12V throttle, forward/reverse. After six months of trial and error in the workshop, it finally runs consistently, so I'm laying PVC-and-wood track at my parents' place in Georgia. No engineering background, plenty of wrong turns, one jig for spacing the rails, and a top-speed test that got a little hairier than planned.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zMOMBH8v: Upgraded my miter saw stand during quarantine — added fold-up wings on piano hinges so I can finally cut long boards without propping them on a scrap 2x4. Made it up as I went: jigsawed around the legs, swapped the warped dumpster plywood for something flatter, notched the wings to clear the saw at 45 degrees, shimmed out the play. Inspired by April Wilkerson's mobile stand build, scaled to my shop. Also figured out why my screws kept stripping — wrong bit size for two and a half years.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=szQMdobw: Shop reset day — swapped the 2700K bulbs for daylight to kill the color temp mismatch in my videos, then dug through two and a half years of accumulated gear deciding what stays. Pulled out resin Mickey ice cream bar molds from a work shoot, a few gloriously bad paintings (including a Back to the Future flux capacitor I'm framing anyway), and the haul from yesterday's dumpster dive at a nearby construction site. Cleaned the nails out of salvaged two-by-fours headed for the dump — about twenty bucks of lumber for ten minutes of work. Reduce, reuse, recycle, and quit throwing perfectly good wood away.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qao0eTyB: Built a floating frame for a portrait gifted by my neighbor, artist Gustavo Loza, with a deliberate one-inch gap so you can still see it was painted on a cardboard box. Designed in SketchUp, ripped the stock down, cut two styles of 45-degree miter joints, and assembled the inner mount with a new pin nailer. Shot and edited entirely on iPhone — my first YouTube video, made to find out if filmmaker brain and maker brain actually play well together.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yOfJLOBp: Bats stream out of a gable vent in Dublin, Georgia the second the water hits — a full colony, not the two or three the homeowner expected. Shot during a routine exterior wash, captured because you never know what's living in the eaves until you flush it out. Stick around for the count.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wKZeYkAf: Tormach featured my journey from quitting Disney to becoming a garage machinist learning CNC on their 15L Slant-PRO Lathe. Proof that a clean career pivot — and a willingness to look dumb on camera — can land you in the manufacturer's own highlight reel.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6oOFdERU: Host and creator of Tormach's eight-part Garage Series — the official onboarding course for new owners of the 1100MX CNC mill. I walk first-time machinists through power-up, the PathPilot interface, workholding, tooling, coolant, CAD/CAM, and conversational programming, then close the series running and inspecting real parts. Built for garage makers and shop teachers who want to be cutting chips fast. Episode one sets the table.
https://vimeo.com/1115834134: Directed and produced Big Fish for the Artios Drama Department — a full-stage musical about Edward Bloom, the father whose tall tales outgrew the truth, and the son trying to find him inside them. Built the world from the ground up: staging, performances, and the emotional throughline that lands legacy, faith, and family without flinching. Tall tales, real tears — press play.

https://vimeo.com/1060278500: Directed and produced this high-energy prequel to Peter Pan — the origin story behind Neverland, Hook, and the boy who wouldn't grow up. The kind of footage that reminds you why people plan years around a single trip.

https://drive.google.com/file/d/1Y64Zpib: Shot and edited a Jungle Book-themed musical bit at a TSA checkpoint — agent asks for ID, scene cuts straight into a full I Wan'na Be Like You performance with choreography, beat drops, and scat breaks. https://drive.google.com/file/d/1pUuIpG_: A 3:30 AM wake-up, a Magical Express ride, and a full-tilt original party track scoring the whole trip. I cut this Disney World vacation video as a music-driven hype piece — every lyric, every beat, every M-I-C-K-E-Y chant landing on a smile, a ride drop, or a fireworks pop. Family memories, edited like a single.
https://drive.google.com/file/d/1Nmx53tn: Rhonda Rolle's sister-in-law Sandy, 70 and disabled, hired a live-in caretaker after losing her husband — and ended up with a squatter who slammed her against a door, stole documents, and threatened to claim the Virginia house as her own. Sandy now lives behind a locked bedroom door in her own home, too afraid to sleep or eat. From Cheyenne, Wyoming, Rhonda is fighting to get her out before adverse possession kicks in. A first-person account of elder abuse hiding behind tenant rights — and the family racing the clock to stop it.
https://drive.google.com/file/d/1ek6bfH6: A first-person account from Janusz Orzechowski, a 71-year-old Polish immigrant whose Tacony retirement property — bought in 2013, the neighborhood now booming after decades of decline — has been overrun by squatters. The story unfolds through a Philadelphia Water Department alert: 96 hours of continuous flow, a plumber dispatched, suspected drug activity in the back hutch, jumped electric, no meter. Janusz lays out the catch-22 with the calm of someone who's done the math — no pension, no union, just Social Security and what he set aside, and a house he can't sell with people inside it. Removing them, he says, would be the best news of the year.
https://drive.google.com/file/d/16fkUzeI: A first-person account from Kevin Ertle, a Bandon, Oregon homeowner whose inherited family property was overrun by people he let in out of kindness. He walks through the wreckage — stolen paintings, a trashed shop, identity theft that drained over $95,000, and threats to his dog Frannie — while local authorities decline to act. Shot as a plea for help getting his home, and his life, back.
https://drive.google.com/file/d/12nOf0c5: A documentary portrait of Cheryl and Ron, a retired Oregon couple trapped in a slow-motion crisis after letting family move into the house next door — a property in their family since the late '40s. What started as compassion for a homeless pregnant granddaughter has spiraled into overdoses, a baby born addicted, $3,000 in legal fees, and police who won't respond. Shot vérité-style with raw kitchen-table interviews, the piece sits with the exhaustion of people who did the right thing and got punished for it. The question it leaves you with isn't what they should do — it's why no one will help.
https://drive.google.com/file/d/1q9LFoqq: Ray Stanley Craig bought a Cleveland house to move his family home and leave something for his kids — then squatters took it over. He flew out from California, posted three-day evictions, changed the locks, and got a "courtesy call" from the city telling him he couldn't. The move is on hold, the bills are climbing, and the law keeps treating him like the problem. A blue-collar guy at his breaking point, asking out loud why the squatters have more rights than he does.
https://drive.google.com/file/d/1qzjvePG: Megan Gibbons inherited her godparents' Newtown Square home in 2019 — the same house she grew up running around in as a kid. In August 2024, weeks after losing her mom, a couple she'd let onto the property jammed her garage with screwdrivers, locked her out, and called the cops on her for trespassing on land she owned. She walked out with two assault charges, two harassment counts, 25 hours of anger management, 80 hours of community service, and restitution owed to the people squatting in her house. She's telling it now so the next person sees the playbook coming.
https://drive.google.com/file/d/1SBn-ftG: Documentary-style interview with Oded and Natasha, an Israeli-American family caught between two homes — a dying grandmother in the States, four kids who barely know her, and a squatter who walked into their American property and refused to leave. Shot vérité, no narration, just the family laying out the impossible math of a $5,000 eviction retainer from 7,000 miles away while cousins are being buried after October 7th. The story holds because they don't perform the stress — they just describe it, and you feel the violation land. A quiet study in what "home" costs when you're trying to hold onto two of them.
https://drive.google.com/file/d/1b19dglT: Director-led school overview produced for Shadowbrook Weekday School, a 40-year ministry of Shadowbrook Church serving kids 15 months through kindergarten. Cut to surface the essentials a parent actually wants — mission, hours, Gwinnett calendar alignment, daily rhythm, specials lineup, and the Zoofonics/Mindful Math/Scholastic curriculum stack — without losing the warmth of Jenny's delivery. Closes on the family events calendar and a direct line to the director, so an interested parent knows exactly what to do next.
